I have learned, and some of you have heard me say this before, but I have learned that when it comes to pricing products like courses or consulting or anything that doesn't have like, here's the thing of Campbell soup sitting on the shelf and you're trying to come out with your own brand of soup that will go directly against them. So, there's a face-to-face, they stand on this shelf and see three products in a line and price them against each other. Anytime you're not in that situation, I have found that what people tend to do when they price their product and the price they choose usually has almost nothing to do with the value that that product gives to the lives of those who buy it. It has nothing to do with how much people want that product or don't want that product. It has nothing to do with what's actually in the product and the transformation that it creates. It has almost everything to do with how people feel about themselves.

## [06:01 - 09:04]

And what I've learned is the fastest way to feel like you're worth $2,000 to sell a product or $1,000 to sell a product is to sell a product for $2,000 or $1,000. And I know that's hard to hear because we had this natural tendency, which is, oh man, no one will buy it at that price. I've got to be lower. And so, and as soon as we set a price that's lower, then we look at that and say, oh, I don't know if anyone will pay that either. I think I need to go lower. And pretty soon we're selling this thing for $19. And that's not... And oftentimes it actually hurts you to be priced too low. People don't take you seriously when you're priced too low. When they see somebody else out there offering a product for $2,000 and you're offering it for $197, they say, well, they must not be as valuable as this $2,000 product out there.
